narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 50% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold we use to make admission decisions. Looking at your profile, you have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which is stronger than the typical applicant's 3.21, along with a middle-family income background, and you're planning to attend full-time as a female White student—demographics that are fairly common among applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile of 3 falls below the average of 5.71, your Year 3 decile of 1 is much lower than the typical 5.53, and your LSAT score of 33 is under the average 36.70, which stood out to the model. When the model evaluated your application, it ranked your Year 3 performance as the most influential factor, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure compared to what it expects for most students. Your LSAT score was the next biggest influence in the same direction, followed closely by your Year 1 grades, with family income also playing a notable role toward that outcome. Other aspects like your racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender had progressively smaller impacts, all nudging slightly toward the failure prediction but not as strongly. Overall, these key academic markers—especially your later-year school performance and LSAT—deviated from the stronger profiles of students the model sees as likely to pass, tipping the balance at that 50% mark. While your undergraduate GPA was a relative bright spot, it wasn't enough to offset those concerns in the model's view. To improve your chances for future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average score, as that's a changeable factor that carries significant weight. You might also reflect on what led to the lower Year 3 decile and seek ways to demonstrate improvement, like through additional coursework or strong recommendation letters highlighting growth. Building a narrative of academic resilience could help reframe your profile for other schools. Keep pushing forward—you have strengths like your GPA that show real potential, and targeted steps can make a big difference next time.
